title: Percentage of a Percentage Calculator - Nested Percentages
tagline: Find what one percentage is of another — instantly solve layered discounts, compound fees, and nested statistical data.
intro: Enter two percentages to calculate the first as a fraction of the second. The result is a percentage of the whole, not just of the second value.

field.p2Hint: This is the 'whole' percentage you are taking a part of.

error.invalid: Please enter valid numbers in both percentage fields.

about.heading: About the percentage of a percentage calculator
about.body:
Calculating a percentage of a percentage — sometimes called a nested percentage — is a surprisingly common task in finance, retail, and statistics, yet it trips up even people who are comfortable with ordinary percentage arithmetic. The core insight is that when you are applying a percentage to another percentage, the two percentages both need to be expressed as fractions of a whole before you multiply them together.

The formula is simple: result = (P1 × P2) ÷ 100. You multiply the two percentage values and divide by 100. This is equivalent to converting each percentage to a decimal (divide each by 100) and then multiplying those decimals together — either approach gives the same answer. For example, 25% of 40% is (25 × 40) ÷ 100 = 10%, meaning that 25% of a quantity that was itself 40% of a larger whole represents 10% of that larger whole.

Retail is probably the most familiar context. When a store runs a 40% off sale and then gives you a coupon for an additional 25% off the original price, the coupon saves you 25% of 40% = 10% of the original price. Note the crucial phrase "of the original price" — if the coupon instead takes 25% off the already-reduced price, the calculation is different and you would need to apply the second discount to the new price, not compute a nested percentage of the original.

In finance, fees are frequently layered. A mutual fund might charge an expense ratio of 2%, and your investment platform might charge a 10% advisory fee on top of that ratio. The platform's fee as a fraction of your total investment is 10% of 2% = 0.2%. Knowing this allows you to compare the true cost of different fund-and-platform combinations on a consistent basis.

Statisticians often need nested percentages when analysing population sub-groups. If 60% of city residents own a pet and 30% of those pet owners have a cat, then cat owners represent 30% of 60% = 18% of the total city population. This kind of layered analysis is central to survey research, epidemiology, and demographic reporting.

A common mistake is to add the two percentages rather than multiply them. A 30% group within a 60% group is not 90% of the total — it is 18%, because you must take the fraction of a fraction, not combine the two fractions into one. The nested-percentage calculator on this page removes the risk of this error by doing the multiplication for you. Enter P1 in the first field, P2 in the second, and the result shows the combined percentage relative to the original whole.

examples.heading: Percentage of a percentage examples
examples.intro: Four real-world scenarios illustrating nested percentage calculations.

items.note: (25 × 40) ÷ 100 = 10%. An extra coupon for 25% off the original price on a 40%-off item saves you 10% of the original price.
(30 × 60) ÷ 100 = 18%. If 60% of residents own pets and 30% of those own cats, cat owners make up 18% of the total population.
(10 × 2) ÷ 100 = 0.2%. An advisor who charges 10% of a fund's 2% expense ratio adds an effective cost of 0.2% of your investment.
(75 × 80) ÷ 100 = 60%. A straightforward example: 75% of a group that represents 80% of the whole equals 60% of the whole.

howto.heading: How to use the percentage of a percentage calculator
steps: Enter the 'part' percentage in the First Percentage field — this is the percentage you want to take of the other.
Enter the 'whole' percentage in the Second Percentage field — this is the percentage that P1 is applied to.
Click Calculate. The result shows P1% of P2% expressed as a percentage of the original whole.
Review the formula shown beneath the result to understand exactly how the calculation was performed.
Click Reset to clear the fields, or tap one of the example buttons to load a pre-filled real-world scenario.

items.q: What is the formula for a percentage of a percentage?
items.a: The formula is result = (P1 × P2) ÷ 100. Multiply the two percentage values together and divide by 100. This is equivalent to converting each to a decimal and multiplying: (P1/100) × (P2/100) × 100 = (P1 × P2)/100.

items.q: Why can't I just add the two percentages together?
items.a: Adding percentages only works when both percentages refer to the same whole. A nested percentage means P1 is applied to a quantity that is itself P2 of a larger whole, so you must multiply the fractions, not sum them. Adding 30% and 60% gives 90%, but the correct answer for 30% of the 60% group is only 18%.

items.q: What is the difference between a percentage of and a percentage point?
items.a: A 'percentage of' is a multiplicative operation — you find a fraction of a fraction. A 'percentage point' is an additive measure of the arithmetic difference between two percentages. Moving from 4% to 5% is a 1 percentage-point change but a 25% increase in the rate. This calculator handles the multiplicative case.

items.q: Can the result be more than 100%?
items.a: Yes, if P1 is greater than 100%. For example, 200% of 60% is (200 × 60) ÷ 100 = 120%. This means the sub-group is larger than the group it was taken from, which is a valid mathematical result even if it sounds unusual in plain language.

items.q: How does this help with layered retail discounts?
items.a: If a product is 40% off and you have a coupon for an additional 25% off the original price, that coupon gives you 25% of 40% = 10% off the original. Your total discount is 40% + 10% = 50%. However, if the coupon is 25% off the sale price (not the original), the extra saving is 25% of the remaining 60%, which is 15% off the original price — a more generous deal.

items.q: Does this calculator work with decimal percentages?
items.a: Yes. You can enter any decimal value such as 2.5 or 0.1. The formula (P1 × P2) ÷ 100 works equally well for whole numbers and decimals. For example, 0.5% of 50% = (0.5 × 50) ÷ 100 = 0.25%.
